coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical sensor based on N,P–doped carbon quantum dots derived from the banana flower bract (Musa acuminata) biomass extract for selective and picomolar detection of dopamine
coredata.dc:creator: Padmapriya, A.
Thiyagarajan, P.
Devendiran, M.
Kalaivani, R.A.
Shanmugharaj, A.M.
coredata.dc:description: A facile route was proposed in preparing phosphorus and nitrogen dual carbon quantum dots (N,P-CQD) from banana flower bract extract by hydrothermal synthesis for selective and reliable detection of catecholamines such as dopamine (DA). By morphologically characterizing the synthesized CQD using Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), it is discovered that its average particle size is 3.8 nm. While the doping of the heteroatoms upsurges the electrical conductivity of the CQD, the presence of the functional sites like acid (–COOH), (–NH2) and phosphate (-PO4 3-) groups selectively attract the cations via., an ion-exchange mechanism leaving behind the anions, due to the electrostatic repulsion. The synthesized N,P-CQD/PIGE electrode-based electrochemical sensors demonstrated high selectivity and sensitivity for DA with a relatively low limit of detection (LOD) (∼500 pM) and a wide linear range, extending from 6.0 μM to 0.1 mM. The N,P-CQD's detection selectivity is further validated by utilizing a combination with a somewhat larger concentration of uric (UA) and ascorbic (AA) acids and only a modest amount of DA. Additionally, the N,P-CQD/PIGE electrode successfully detects DA with a LOD as low as 630 pM and a larger linear range of 2.5 M to 0.16 mM in real-time samples of dopamine injection.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of pristine, single, and dual-doped carbon quantum dots from banana bract biomass extract using a hydrothermal approach.
(i) FTIR spectrum of (a) crude, (b) pristine, (c) nitrogen, (d) phosphorus, and (e) nitrogen and phosphorus dual carbon quantum dots. Raman shift of (ii) CQD and (iii) N,P-CQD.
High-resolution XPS spectra of N,P-CQD (i) carbon - C1s, (ii) oxygen - O1s, (iii) nitrogen - N1s, and (iv) and phosphorous - P2p.
(i) XRD spectra of (a) CQD, (b) N-CQD, (c) P-CQD, and (d) N,P-CQD, (ii) TEM image of N,P-CQD, Inset (SEAD & HRTEM image), (iii) d-spacing, and (iv) Particle size histogram of N,P-CQD.

(i) UV spectra of (a) crude, (b) CQD, (c) N-CQD, (d) P-CQD and (e) N,P-CQD, (Inset The pictures of N,P-CQD in daylight (f) and UV light (g) (ii) PL spectra of N,P-CQD at different excitation wavelengths from 300 to 480 nm.

(i) The CV responses of bare PIGE (curve a), CQD & modified CQD PIGE (curves b-e) in 0.1 M KCl solution containing 0.1 M of [Fe (CN)6 3-/4-] measured at the scan rate of 50 mVs−1. (ii) EIS responses of bare PIGE (curve a), CQD & modified CQD/PIGE (curves b-e) in 0.1 M KCl electrolytic solution containing 0.1 M of Fe(CN)6 3-/4 (Inset: EIS response curves in the scale range of 0 – 2 kohms).

The CV responses of N,P-CQD modified PIGE electrode for varying concentration (i) from 2 μM to 0.2 mM in 0.1 M PBS at pH = 7. (ii) Calibration graph for Ipa (red) and Ipc (black). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Chronoamperometry response of the NP-CQD/PIGE modified electrode at dynamic conditions at 0.2 V (i) for the successive addition of DA. (ii) calibration graph in low and high concentrations of DA from 2 to 20 μM and 57 to 0.15 mM respectively in 0.1 M PBS at pH 7.
The DPV response of N,P-CQD modified electrode (Inset - the calibration graph) for the concentration range varied from 6 μM to 0.1 mM, in 0.1 M PBS at pH 7.

The DPVs were recorded using an N,P-CQD/PIGE electrode in 0.1 M PBS at pH = 7 containing (i) 1 μM UA and AA with varying DA concentration from 5 to 60 μM, (ii) 1 μM UA and DA with varying AA concentration from 5 to 100 μM, and (iii) 1 μM AA and DA with varying UA concentration from 5 to 100 μM.
Schematic illustration of electro-oxidation of DA in N,P-CQD/PIGE modified electrode.
Schematic representation of chemical binding of DA with (i) PIGE (bare) and (ii) with N, P-CQD/PIGE modified electrode.
The EIS data of the Bare, CQD, N-CQD, P-CQD, and N,P-CQD-modified electrodes.
Comparison of LOD and Linear Range of different electrode materials using electrochemical techniques CV and DPV.

head.ce:title: Electrochemical sensor based on N,P–doped carbon quantum dots derived from the banana flower bract (Musa acuminata) biomass extract for selective and picomolar detection of dopamine

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Paraffin impregnated graphite (PIGE) based electrochemical sensor fabricated using banana bract extract derived N, P-doped carbon quantum dots (N, P-CQDs) selectively detect the dopamine neurotransmitter at the picomolar level concentrations.

A facile route was proposed in preparing phosphorus and nitrogen dual carbon quantum dots (N,P-CQD) from banana flower bract extract by hydrothermal synthesis for selective and reliable detection of catecholamines such as dopamine (DA). By morphologically characterizing the synthesized CQD using Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), it is discovered that its average particle size is 3.8 nm. While the doping of the heteroatoms upsurges the electrical conductivity of the CQD, the presence of the functional sites like acid (–COOH), (–NH2) and phosphate (-PO4 3-) groups selectively attract the cations via., an ion-exchange mechanism leaving behind the anions, due to the electrostatic repulsion. The synthesized N,P-CQD/PIGE electrode-based electrochemical sensors demonstrated high selectivity and sensitivity for DA with a relatively low limit of detection (LOD) (∼500 pM) and a wide linear range, extending from 6.0 μM to 0.1 mM. The N,P-CQD's detection selectivity is further validated by utilizing a combination with a somewhat larger concentration of uric (UA) and ascorbic (AA) acids and only a modest amount of DA. Additionally, the N,P-CQD/PIGE electrode successfully detects DA with a LOD as low as 630 pM and a larger linear range of 2.5 M to 0.16 mM in real-time samples of dopamine injection.

ce:section.ce:para: The human brain's neural networks process enormous amounts of information from the environment via senses including hearing, touch, and sight, which are then integrated with signals throughout the body [1]. Neurotransmitters (NTs), which are endogenous chemical messengers, are utilized by neurons to communicate with one another, affect muscle cells, or trigger a response in glandular cells. Human neurotransmitters mostly fall into the categories of amino acids (such as tyrosine and glutamic acid), biogenic amines (such as serotonin epinephrine, dopamine, and norepinephrine), and soluble gases (for ex., hydrogen sulfide, and nitric oxide) [2]. A catecholamine neurotransmitter, DA, is necessary to develop the mammalian central nervous system and hormones. When DA level is below normal can cause several neurological conditions, such as Huntington's disease, Parkinson's disease, and schizophrenia [3]. Many researchers are very interested in developing techniques and measuring catecholamine NTs since they may be utilizedas therapeutically relevant biomarkers for particular illness states or to monitor the efficacy of therapies. DA concentrations can be measured in a range of biological fluids, including urine,platelets, saliva, serum, plasma, and cerebral spine fluids because NTs are found throughout the body [4]. As a result, quantitative assessment of NTsin various human systems is critical for screening, illness monitoring, and therapeutic approaches. (see Scheme 1. )

With their low cost, ease of miniaturization, ease of use, greater sensitivity, selectivity, speed, stability, dependability, and accuracy, electrochemical techniques stood out as having a quick response time [5]. Recent breakthroughs in nanotechnology aided in the blooming of innovative electrochemical sensors for DA determination that include the incorporation of nanoparticles [6], or graphene (GR) derivatives on an electrode surface [7], mesoporous carbons [8], carbon nanotubes (CNT) [9], and complex nanocomposites [10], using various nanomaterials and/or additional electrocatalysts utilized in the production of modified electrodes. The use of chemically modified electrodes (CME) is growing as a result of their advantages in terms of less challenging manufacturing techniques, increased electrochemical catalytic activity, and physical toughness [11]. Notably, DA coexists in extracellular fluids with other interfering substances such as tryptophan (Trp), ascorbic acid (AA), and uric acid (UA). The electrochemical method attracted attention because of its quick detection, simplicity, repeatability, excellent cost-effectiveness, and miniaturizationpotential [10].

In South India, bananas are grown on more than one lakh hectares, and this harvesting produces more than 10 million tons of bio-waste annually. The objective is to manage this enormous amount of bio-waste. Culinary banana bract is a dietary fiber (DF) by-product of banana farming that is rich in antioxidants. Owing to its numerous health advantages, especially in preventing and treating chronic and degenerative diseases, this antioxidant DF is extremely substantial [12]. As a result, it makes sense to use food waste as a starting material in the CQD preparation. The composition of chemicals, anti-bacterial, and anti-oxidant capacity properties of the bracts of bananas (Musa acuminata) were studied. The outcome of the imminent analysis showed a significant amount of raw protein, raw fiber, fat content, and carbohydrate in Musa acuminata. The cellulose and lignin content of the bract sample M. acuminata is estimated as (34.61 ± 1.06%; 9.13 ± 0.31%). Hence, M. Acuminata is chosen as a biomass precursor [13].

CQD is a new emerging carbonaceous nanomaterial that has piqued the research community owing to its beneficial properties. Usually, CQD is an advantageous substitute for semiconductor quantum dots in biological and chemical analysisbecause they typically have few valuable properties, such as fluorescence, biocompatibility, phenomenal up conversion, exceptional photostability, less toxicity, and are typically less than 10 nm in size [14], CQD is frequently formed on macroscopic carbon structures using either chemical or physical cutting processes such as graphite, carbon nanotubes, carbon powders, and carbon fibers [15]. Hydrothermal and microwave treatment of organic precursors is another option for CQD synthesis [16,17]. When compared to other synthetic techniques, the hydrothermal process is straightforward, affordable, and capable of producing a range of CQD in a single step. CQD disperses efficiently in water due to the presence of hydroxy, carboxyl, and epoxy groups on its surface. These functional groups not only give excellent dispersibility in water but also provide photoluminescent (PL) CQD. Finally, surface modification using heteroatom doping can boost the quantum yield of CQD [18]. In addition, by controlling the size of CQD via the quantum detention effect, one may control the electromagnetic, optical, and electrochemical properties [19]. CQD research has recently generated a lot of enthusiasm due to its apparent promise in analytical applications [20-22].

In the present study, the one-step hydrothermal aided technique was adopted for the synthesis of pristine and heteroatom (N,P-CQD, N-CQD, and P-CQD) doped carbon quantum dots from banana bract extract. To understand the structural, morphological, and chemical structures of the prepared CQDs, various characterization techniques such as UV–Visible spectroscopy, X-ray diffractometer (XRD), Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR), Raman spectroscopy, High-resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HR-TEM) and X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) were used. Subsequently, electrochemical sensors based on paraffin-impregnated graphite electrode (PIGE) was fabricated to detect dopamine, which was further characterized using various electrochemical tools such as Cyclic Voltammetry (CV), Chronoamperometry (CA), and Differential Pulse Voltammetry (DPV). Hetero atom doped carbon quantum dot (N,P-CQD) modified PIGE electrode detects ultralow concentrations (in picomolar) of dopamine, which is the first of its kind, and to date, no report has been made using paraffin-impregnated graphite (PIGE) electrodes.

Sigma Aldrich supplied the graphite rods. SRL chemicals, India supplied phosphoric acid and ethylene diamine. The analytes such as uric acid (UA), ascorbic acid (AA), and dopamine (DA) were procured from Himedia laboratories, India. Background electrolytes were prepared using double-distilled water before each experiment, and purified nitrogen gas was purged to eradicate dissolved oxygen.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of pristine, single, and dual-doped carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The carbon source, banana flower bract was collected from a banana tree that had been planted in the house and repeatedly cleansed with tap and deionized water (Fig. 1 ). This was crushed in a household juicer with distilled water, and the extract was filtered using Whatmanfilter paper and frozen for later use. The hydrothermal reaction was carried out in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave using 50 mL of the extract for six hours at 180 °C and subsequently allowed to cool naturally to ambient temperature [23,24]. The resultant brownish-black solution was centrifuged at 2000 rpm for 30 min followed by a cooling centrifuge at 8000 rpm at −4°C for 15 min. The filtrate was then frozen at −4°C and kept for further study after being microfiltered using 0.2 mm membrane filter paper to remove any solid contaminants. The same procedure was employed again for Nitrogen-doped (N-CQD), Phosphorous-doped (P-CQD), and phosphorous and nitrogen dual-doped (N,P-CQD) quantum dots using 1 mL of Ethylenediamine and 1 mL of Phosphoric acid which act as nitrogen and phosphorous precursors, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of N, P modified CQD electrode
ce:section.ce:para: The paraffin wax was impregnated on a bare graphite electrode (PIGE). The PIGE was prepared as previously stated [25]. The PIGE's surface was first hoovered and polished with abrasive paper, then treated in a 0.5 m alumina slurry, rinsed with distilledwater, and allowed to air-dry in a nitrogen atmosphere. To modify the electrode surface, the as-prepared carbon quantum dots (pristine and doped) was used as a stock solution. 2 μL of the CQD dispersion was drop cast on the polished PIGE surface and was left to dry in ambient conditions. After surface coating, the electrode was gently rinsed with water to remove any unbound CQD from the surface, then dried before use.

X-ray diffractometer (Smart Lab, Rigaku Inc. Ltd Japan) equipped with copper source (CuKα, λ = 1.54056 A°, set at 0.04 MV and 0.03 μA) was utilized for obtaining X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the sample. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, JEOL JEM 2100+, Japan) was used to examine the morphological features of the prepared CQDs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) characterization of the synthesized CQDs was done in Versaprobe III, PHI instruments (PHI5000; ULVAC-PHI Inc. Ltd., Japan). UV–Vis spectra were acquired using a UV–VIS Spectrophotometer (Model-JAZ; Make-Ocean optics, USA). The Raman spectrum of the synthesized CQDs was determined using a Confocal Raman Microscope-Imaging/Spectrometer (Model-Alpha 300R, WITEC, GmbH Germany). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopic (FTIR) characterization was carried out in the spectrum-one FT-IR spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer Inc. Ltd., USA) under diffuse reflectance mode kept at the resolution of 0.4 cm−1 in the wavenumber range of 4000–400 cm−1. Photoluminescence spectra were done using JASCO FP-650 spectrofluorometer (Japan) in a 1 cm quartz cell and a thermostat bath.

All the Electrochemical studies were obtained using a potentiostat/galvanostat (760A, CH instruments, USA) and a three-electrode system encompassing a CQD modified PIGE as the working electrode, platinum wire as the counter electrode and Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode. Cyclic voltammetry (CV), Chrono-amperometry (CA), and Differential pulse voltammetry techniques (DPV) were adopted for electrochemical measurements for the DA analysis at pH 7 using PBS buffer solution as the supporting electrolyte, set at the scan rate of 50 mV/s. All pH measurements were performed employing a Thermo Scientific A215 instrument.

Generally, CQD derived from bio-mass carbon sources have meager fluorescencequalities because of the lower heteroatom (such as N) concentration in the molecular backbone. Also, heteroatom doping techniques are an efficient method to effectively manipulate the optical and electronic properties of the CQD. Furthermore, doping of heteroatoms as well as surface functionalization of the CQD have a greater impact on the enhancement of their electrochemical properties. In terms of electron transfer, the integration of electron-donating heteroatoms (for example, nitrogen) might result in a higher rate of heterogeneous electron transfer (HET), whereas doping with electron-drawing heteroatoms (e.g., halogens) results in lessened HET rates. Increased oxygen functional groups on CQD may potentially impair the conductive sp2 carbon network, delaying electron transit [26]. Doping of dual/multi-elements on the CQD surface has been thoroughly studied recently because of their significant synergistic impact. Thus, in the present investigation, an attempt has been made to synthesize multi-element (N, P) doped CQDs using banana bract extract as the carbon source, ethylene diamine, and phosphoric acid as the source for nitrogen and phosphorus respectively (Fig. 1). The synthesized CQD were characterized using various tools as discussed below.

FT-IR spectroscopy was used to identify the surface functional sites in the biomass-derived pristine and doped CQD, and the findings are shown in Fig. 2 (i). For evaluation purposes, the FT-IR result of the banana bract (crude) sample was also performed and the result is displayed in Fig. 2(ia). Banana bract samples displayed several IR peaks corresponding to –OH stretching (∼3200–3400 cm−1), –CH stretching (∼2900 cm−1), aromatic CC stretching (∼1630 cm−1), organic sulfates (∼1464 cm−1), –CH bending (∼1430 cm−1), Si-O-Si stretching (∼1187 cm−1), CO stretching (∼1022 cm−1) and –CH wagging of aromatic rings (∼810 cm−1). In contrast to the banana bract crude sample, pure and doped CQD have a significantly lesser number of IR active bands (Fig. 2 (ii(b-e)). For instance, pristine CQD displayed three major IR transitions at ∼ 3315 cm−1 (–OH stretching), 1630 cm−1 (aromatic CC stretching), and 1075 cm−1 (CO stretching) confirming the formation of CQD with –OH functional sites at its backbone. Alternatively, two IR bands, observed at 3346 cm−1 (–OH stretching) and at 3260 cm−1 (–NH stretching) along with the peak corresponding to aromatic CC stretching (∼1630 cm−1) and confirms the presence of both oxygen and nitrogen functionalities in the synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CQD). Contrastingly, new IR peaks at 2100 cm−1 (–CN stretching), 1245 cm−1 (OPO stretching), 1156 cm−1 (PO stretching), and at 1000 cm−1 (PO stretching of POR, where R is the aromatic group) in addition to –OH (∼3300 cm−1) and CC stretching (∼1630 cm−1) have appeared in phosphorous doped carbon dots corroborating the introduction of phosphate groups on the CQD surface. Co-doping of nitrogen and phosphorus on the carbon dot surface (N,P-CQD) resulted in the weak IR bands at 2100 cm−1 (–CN stretching), 1243 cm−1 (OPO stretching), 1154 cm−1 (PO stretching), 1075 cm−1 (CO stretching) and 933 cm−1 (PO stretching of POR, where R is the aromatic group) along with the peaks corresponding to –OH (∼3300 cm−1) and CC stretching (∼1630 cm−1) vibrations, revealing the successful formation of N,P doped CQDs (Fig. 2) [27,28]. In addition, P-CQD, and N,P-CQD exhibit weak broad transitions in the spectral range of ∼ 470–515 cm−1 corroborating the presence of phosphate moieties (-PO4 3-) in both samples. The findings revealed that the synthesized N,P-CQD were packed with diverse functional groups, which might increase their aqueous solubility for prospective applications in bio-imaging and sensing sectors.

The chemical active sites and the functional groups present on the N,P-CQDs were corroborated using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) characterizations, and the resultant outcomes are displayed in Fig. 3 . Survey scan findings of N,P-CQD is shown in Fig. S1 in the ESI are which demonstrated the presence of phosphorus (P2p), oxygen (O1s), Carbon (C1s) and Nitrogen (N1s) atoms. The relative atomic concentration (χ) of nitrogen (N1s) and phosphorous (P2p) were calculated using the following expression [29],
(1)
χ =
I p
S p
∑ j
I j
S j
where IP and SP are the intensity and sensitivity factors of a particular element. Alternatively, Ij and Sj are the intensity and sensitivity factors of various elements present in the N, P-CQD. The relative atomic concentration of nitrogen and phosphorus calculated using the above expression is observed to be 16.04% and 2.67%, respectively. The functional groups present in the N,P-CQDs were further corroborated by deconvoluting high-resolution XPS results of carbon, oxygen, nitrogen, and phosphorous, and the results are included in Fig. 3 (i-iv). Deconvolution of C1s peak displayed five peaks that are correlated to CC/CH (∼284.7 eV; ∼57 %), CO/CN/CP (∼286.1 eV; ∼27.6 %), COC (∼286.7 eV; ∼12.5 %) and -CO (∼288 eV; ∼3 %) groups, respectively. A higher concentration of the COC group (∼29.7 %) revealed that the free radical scavenging reactions are easier in the prepared CQD [30]. Upon deconvolution N1s spectra showed three peaks supportive to pyridinic (∼398.8 eV), pyrrolic (∼399.6 eV), and graphitic (∼400.5 eV) nitrogen, corroborating the introduction of nitrogen atoms in the hexagonally packed crystalline graphitic structures [31]. On the contrary, deconvolution of the high-resolution spectrum of phosphorus resulted in two peaks consistent to PC (∼132.7 eV) and PO (∼133.5 eV) groups corroborating the presence of phosphate groups as the dangling moieties on the CQD surface [32]. Furthermore, the deconvolution of O1s resulted in the appearance of peaks matching to -CO (∼530.6 eV), PO (∼532.5 eV), and CO (∼533 eV) confirming the presence of oxygen moieties bonded between the carbon and phosphorus atoms in the resultant CQD. Based on the chemical characterization results, the proposed atomic configuration of the prepared N,P-CQD is revealed in Fig. S2 in the ESI.

The structural revelations of the synthesized carbon quantum dots (CQD) were investigated using X-ray diffraction (XRD) and Raman spectroscopy and the observations are included in Fig. 4 (i) and 2 (ii & iii) respectively. Included in Fig. 4 i (a-d) is the XRD patterns of undoped (CQD), nitrogen-doped (N-CQD), phosphorus-doped (P-CQD), and nitrogen and phosphorus-dual atom doped carbon quantum dots (N,P-CQD) respectively. The distribution of graphitic planes in the puddle of amorphous carbon is demonstrated by the fact that all samples have a substantially wider diffraction pattern at 2θ = 23°, indicating that the material is turbostratic. Relatively broad and weaker (002) peaks for all the doped systems revealed the generation of more surface defects relative to the pristine CQDs (Fig. 4i(a)). To get more insights into the crystalline characteristics, deconvolution of the XRD peaks were done by adopting the Lorentzian fit which renders two peaks corresponding to disordered (21° ∼ 23°) and graphitic (25 ∼ 30°) carbon phases in the resultant CQD [33]. The phase fraction calculated using the area under the peaks of graphitic and disordered carbon phases is tabulated in Table S1 in the ESI. The disordered to ordered phase ratio (PD/PG) is noted to be 3.13 (CQD), 0.7 (N-CQD), 5.13 (P-CQD), and 1.37 for (N,P- CQD) respectively, which revealed the fact that significant changes in the graphitic crystalline phases where due to the doping of nitrogen or phosphorus atoms on the CQD surface [34]. On doping nitrogen into CQD, it gets introduced on the carbon skeleton resulting higher degree of graphitic phases in relevance to the pristine CQD. On the other hand, phosphorus doping resulted in the formation of dangling bonds of the phosphate group without much variation in the carbon skeleton as that of pristine CQD. Interestingly, co-doping of nitrogen and phosphorus resulted in the increment of both graphitic and dangling phosphate bonds in the N,P-CQD samples, which is further evidenced by the PD/PG ratio. Additionally, using Bragg's equation, the average d-spacing values of disordered and graphitic carbons were computed and the result outcomes are presented in Table S1 in the ESI. The average d-spacing values of disordered carbon are observed to be 0.443 (CQD), 0.432 (N-CQD), 0.396 (P-CQD), and 0.393 nm (N,P-CQD), respectively. Likewise, average d-spacing values of graphitic carbon phases are noted to be 0.34 (CQD), 0.37 (N-CQD), 0.36 (P-CQD), and 0.34 nm (N,P-CQD) respectively tabulated in Table S1 in the ESI. Noticeable decrement in the average d-spacing especially in the disordered carbon phase due to the doping of heteroatoms reveals the systematic growth in the graphitic planes, however, these results are somewhat contradict to the phase ratio (PD/PG) values and this fact may be attributed to the variation in the surface defects generated due to the doping of heteroatoms on the CQD surface.

The structural characteristics of the CQD are further validated using Raman spectroscopic characterization of the selected samples (CQD & N,P-CQD), and the results are included in Fig. 2 (ii & iii). Both the samples displayed two Raman bands that correspond to the defective sp2 lattice (∼1350 cm−1) and non-defective sp2 graphitic layer (1585 cm−1) respectively. The respective ID/IG values are noted to be 0.964 (CQD) and 0.86 (N,P-CQD) corroborating the substantial variation in the defective phases due to the doping of heteroatoms on the CQD surface [28]. Engineering the surface defects on the CQD surfaces due to doping is crucial for the electrochemical responses as the reactions are likely to have occurred in the defective sites.

Analysis of morphological features of (N,P-CQD) was performed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and the result is included in Fig. 4 (ii - iv). From the image, one can visualize that the synthesized N,P-CQD are monodisperse and has sphere-shaped morphology. The histogram results further revealed the formation of small size N,P-CQD (∼3.5 nm) with narrow size distribution (Fig. 4 (iv)) [28]. Also, the Lattice fringes (Inset Fig. 4 (ii)), corresponding to the (002) facet of graphite were observed to be 0.343 nm as shown in Fig. 4 (iii), which corroborates the XRD results.

The optical properties of the pristine (CQD) and single and dual doped carbon dots (N-CQD, P-CQD, and N, P-CQD) were investigated using UV–Vis spectroscopy in the spectral range of 200 ∼ 800 nm, and the absorption spectra are included in Fig. 5 (i). In contrast to the crude banana bract sample, pristine CQD showed a weak absorption band at around 277 nm and a diffused hump at ∼ 318 nm, revealing the presence of CC (
π - π
* transitions) and CO (
n - π
* transitions) functional sites on its surface. Similarly, nitrogen and phosphorus-doped carbon dots showed absorption bands induced by the
π - π
* transitions at ∼ 275 nm (N-CQD) and 286 nm (PCQD), respectively. In addition, a weak diffused hump peak was observed at ∼ 305 (N-CQD) and 324 nm (P-CQD), confirming the
n - π
* transitions in these systems. In contrast to the single-doped systems, N,P-CQD clearly demonstrated two absorption peaks at ∼ 277 (
π - π
*) and ∼ 336 nm (
n - π
*) corroborating the presence of both the conjugated structures [28]. The photographic images of the CQD under (i) visible light and (ii) UV light illumination are shown in Fig. 5 (i)'s inset. Under UV illumination (365 nm), the color of all CQD dispersions is bright blue due to the Tyndall effect, whereas it appears as a dark brown color under visible or daylight (∼400 nm).

The absorption, excitation, and emission spectra of the pristine CQD and N,P-CQD are included in Fig. S3 in the ESI. The quantity of particles excited determines the intensity of the PL spectra at a specific wavelength. In the case of pristine CQD, the maximum emission intensity is detected at 448 nm, when exciting the particles at the wavelength of 364 nm, corroborating the fact that a maximum number of particles get excited at this wavelength (∼364 nm). On the other hand, the peak intensity of emission spectra for N,P-CQD appeared at 466 nm (∼4.0 % increment in comparison to pristine CQD) on exciting the particles at the wavelength of 388 nm (∼6.6 % increment in comparison to pristine CQD) [35]. The bathochromic shift in both the emission and excitation wavelength of the N,P-CQD is ascribed to the generation of surface defects due to the doping mechanism, which introduced various surface emissive traps with a concomitant effect, thereby influencing its luminescence properties. Included in Fig. 5 (ii) is the variation in the peak position of the emission spectra on varying the excitation wavelength from 300 ∼ 480 nm for the N,P-CQD. As expected, the peak position of the emission undergoes redshift with increasing excitation wavelength due to the formation a of series of emissive traps between the π-π* transitions and the surface functionalities present in the N,P-CQD surface, resulting in radiative recombination reactions of the surface-confined electrons and holes [35].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical studies of bare and CQD-modified PIGE electrode

ce:section.ce:para: The behaviour of the modified electrode featuring doped carbon quantum dots was investigated using the CV technique. Fig. 6 (i) shows the cyclic voltammogram of (a) bare, (b) CQD, (c) N- CQD, (d) P-CQD, and (e) N,P-CQD modified electrodes, recorded in the potential range of −0.6 V to + 0.8 V at a scan rate of 50 mVs−1 using 0.1 M potassium chloride (KCl) containing 0.1 M of ferricyanide ion (Fe(CN)6 3-/4-). Two reversible redox peaks have been observed for the oxidation and reduction reactions of undoped and doped CQD respectively in the presence of Fe(CN)6 3-/4- medium. The anodic and cathodic potentials of bare and CQD modified PIGE electrodes (CQD, N-CQD, P-CQD, and N,P-CQD) are 0.378 V/-0.156 V (bare PIGE), 0.42 V/-0.135 V (CQD/PIGE), 0.319 V/-0.147 V (NCQD/PIGE), 0.397 V/-0.192 V (PCQD/PIGE), and 0.187 V/0.058 V (N, P-CQD/PIGE) respectively. The difference in the anodic and cathodic potential values (ΔEp = Epa-Epc) are noted to be 0.534 (bare PIGE), 0.555 (CQD/PIGE), 0.449 (N-CQD/PIGE), 0.589 (P-CQD/PIGE), and 0.129 V (N,P-CQD/PIGE) for all the electrodes, respectively. In addition, noticeable increments in the anodic/cathodic peak current values are observed for N,P-CQD/PIGE electrode (14.78 μA/16.22 μA) in comparison to the bare PIGE electrode (4.13 μA/4.59 μA), indicating upliftment in the conductivity values of PIGE [36]. This confirms the presence of N,P-CQD compound on the electrode surface, exhibiting better reversible redox properties. Doping of hetero atoms such as nitrogen (N-CQD) or phosphorus (P-CQD) on the CQD surface enhanced the detection current, however, the effect is more pronounced in the dual system (N,P-CQD) and this fact could be ascribed to the strong synergistic contribution of N,P-CQD, magnifying the electrode's surface area and accelerated electron transfer rate. Hence, N,P-CQD-modified electrode could be a potential candidate for detecting various analytes through the electrochemical method.

The EIS of bare PIGE, CQD/PIGE, and its doped derivatives (N-CQD/PIGE, P-CQD/PIGE, and N,P-CQD/PIGE) was measured in 0.1 M Fe(CN)6 3-/4- to investigate the electron transfer and conductivity properties of the CQD electrodes (Fig. 6 (ii)). The typical Nyquist plot results are shown in Fig. 6 (ii) for all the samples, which displayed a semi-circle region at the high-frequency region and a straight line at the low-frequency region. The impedance information was adjusted to the Randles circuit model as shown in Fig. 6 (ii) (inset)'s and the various parameters such as electrolyte resistance (Rs), charge transfer resistance (Rct), double layer capacitance (Cdl), and Warburg impedance (Zw) values were calculated and the results are incorporated in Table 1 . Noticeable decrement in the charge transfer resistance is observed on doping hetero atoms such as nitrogen (N-CQD, ∼26 %) or phosphorus (P-CQD, ∼82.4 %), whereas co-doping of nitrogen and phosphorus resulted in a drastic decrement in the Rct values (N,P-CQD; ∼98 %). According to the results, the doped material appreciably reduces electron transfer resistance and enhances the electron transport process [37].

The equation (2) given below was used to compute the exchange current density (j0) for the electrode’s electron transfer process from the acquired Rct values.
(2)
j 0
=
RT
nFA
R
ct
where n is the number of electrons that participated in the redox reaction (1), R is the gas constant (8.314 JK1mol), T is the temperature (298 K), F is the Faraday constant (96,485 Cmol−1), and A is the electrode's surface area. The j0 values are observed to be 8.97 μAcm−2 (bare PIGE), 10.2 μAcm−2 (CQD/PIGE), 13.5 μAcm−2 (N-CQD), 57.8 μAcm−2 (P-CQD), and 958 μAcm−2 (N,P-CQD), respectively. Lower Rct and greater j0 values suggest that N,P-CQD is a superior electrochemical sensing platform for analyte detection [38].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical sensing studies of DA with N,P-CQD modified electrode using, differential pulse voltammetry (DPV), chronoamperometry (CA), and cyclic voltammetry (CV)
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the preliminary electrochemical characterization studies (CV and EIS), N,P-CQD/PIGE electrodes was selected for electrochemical sensing characterizations of neurotransmitters such as dopamine, and the techniques such as differential pulse voltammetry (DPV), chronoamperometry (CA), and cyclic voltammetry (CV) have been employed to substantiate their sensing behaviour. Included in Fig. S4 in the ESI are the cyclic voltammograms of bare (curves a and c), and N,P-CQD modified electrodes (curves b and d) in the presence and absence of dopamine (DA). At a scan rate of 50 mVs−1, the voltage was swept between −0.3 and 0.6 V in 0.1 M phosphate buffer (PBS) containing 0.2 µM DA. From the results, one can visualize that the presence of DA had no discernible effect on the current responsiveness of the bare electrode. On the other hand, N,P-CQD modified electrode, when exposed to DA renders outstanding redox characteristics with the peak potential values of 0.18 V (oxidation) and 0.08 V (reduction), respectively. These peak potential values of N,P-CQD/PIGE electrodes for the oxidation of DA are significantly lower in comparison to the other composite electrodes [39]. In addition, enhanced electrocatalytic responses of the N, P-CQD film is further evident by the 16 % increment in the current intensity, when compared to the bare PIGE electrode. The possible redox mechanism of DA induced in the bare PIGE electrode surface and N,P-CQD/PIGE electrode is illustrated in Scheme 2 . DA molecules were oxidized by releasing two electrons, producing dopamine quinone (DQ) during the oxidation reaction, which subsequently gets reduced resulting in the regeneration of dopamine during the reduction reaction as proposed in Scheme 2. The presence of the negatively charged functional sites such as –OH, -PO4 3- as well as electron-rich centres (pyridinic-N & pyrrolic-N) on the N,P-CQD enhanced the electrocatalytic activity, as this negatively charged N,P-CQD modified electrode is more potent towards the positively charged DA (pKa = 8.9) in the PBS buffer medium (pH 7.0) [40]. The negatively charged functional sites chemically bind the dopamine as shown in the schematic representation (Scheme 2), which promotes the redox reaction relatively much faster, thus making it a potent candidate for sensing dopamine.

Furthermore, the effect of electrolyte, pH, and scan rate on the electrocatalytic properties of N,P-CQD/PIGE electrode was examined using CV, which demonstrates the electrochemical reactions on the electrode surface shown in the ESI (Fig. S5 & S6). A hybrid kinetic process is predicted from the relationship between redox peak currents and scan rates. The peak current values are proportional to the square root of the scan rate at lower scan rates (between 0.002and 0.09 V/s), where the electrochemical redox behavior of DA at the N,P-CQDelectrode surface is a diffusion-controlled process. The relationship between redox peak currents and log. scan rates are linear at higher scan rates (greater than200 mV/s), suggesting a surface absorption-controlled mechanism. These findings suggest that the N,P-CQD film hadenough time to function as a three-dimensional electrode, contributing significantly from species diffusing within thin layers, and that DA may easily diffuse through the film at low scan rates. Contrastingly, at high sweep rates, there is not enough time for DA to diffuse through the N,P-CQDlayer, leading to surface adsorption behavior during the electrochemical oxidation process. It is anticipated that the reaction takes place both at the electrode's outer surface and at reactive locations within the assembled adsorbed material [41].

Fig. 7 (i) depicts the CV results of a PIGE/N,P-CQD modified electrode in 0.1 M PBS at pH 7 with varying DA concentrations. The oxidation peak currents rose linearly at 0.17 V with each subsequent injection of DA from 2 μM to 0.2 mM. A Notable blue shift in the oxidation potential of DA is observed with increasing DA concentration due to the variation in the dispersion states and thereby the mass transfer. However, after the saturation limit, a non-linear response in the current value with concentration is observed. The constructed N,P-CQD/PIGE electrode exhibits a linear correlation coefficient in both the low and higher concentrations as shown in Fig. 7 (ii). The
I
pa
= 0.9497 x + 35.04 μ A
with R2 = 0.9967; LOD calculated using the formula 3.29 σ/m, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank response and m is the slope, resulting at a limit of detection (LOD) of 600 pM in a low concentration range from 2 μM to 20 μM (n = 10) [42,43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Amperometric response of DA in the electrochemical oxidation of DA
ce:section.ce:para:
Illustrated in Fig. 8 (i) are the amperometric observations of the N,P-CQD/PIGE electrode toward the consecutive injections of DA (2–150 μM) at a potential of + 0.2 V under hydrodynamic conditions and a swirling speed of 300 rpm/min. With a reaction time of around two seconds, a series of 2 μM DA spikes causea steady rise in the value of the current up to 150 μM of DA. The current response increased on to the consecutive addition of DA. According to scheme 2, DA molecules were oxidized at the surface of an N,P–CQD/PIGE electrode in a solution of 0.1 M PBS. The release of electrons at the electrode surface caused by the oxidation of DA raises the sensor's current. The corresponding calibration plot for various DAconcentrations is shown in Fig. 8 (ii). This calibration plot with
I
pa
= 0.0626 x - 53.82 n A
with R2 value of 0.98 (n = 13), and
I
pa
= 0.1566 x + 5.771 μ A
, R2 = 0.99 (n = 13) with a LOD calculated to be 9241 pM (9.24 nm) and 3716 pM (3.72 nM) in low and high concentrations respectively confirm the excellent linearity of the as-fabricated DAsensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical determination of DA using DPV technique
ce:section.ce:para:
The differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) responses of the N, P-CQD modified electrode after incubation in a DA solution of concentration ranging from 6 μM to 0.1 mM at pH = 7 in PBS buffer within the potential window of 0 to 0.4 V is shown in Fig. 9 . The peak potential is ∼ 0.15 V at low concentration, which results in the blue shift as the concentration is increased. Additionally, the peak current increases with the DA concentration and the oxidation peak current (Ipa) exhibits two linear relationships in the DA concentration ranging 6 μM to 0.1 mM (Fig. 9 (Inset)). The regression analysis of two linear ranges are
I
pa
= 1.168 x + 27.49 μ A
(R2 = 0.99) (6 μM to 25 μM) and
I
pa
= 0.3115 x + 50.78 μ A
(R2 = 0.997) (30 μM to 0.1 mM), respectively. The lowest detection limit (LOD) [42] calculated using the DPV technique is ∼ 500 pM (0.5 nM), which is notably lower when compared to the earlier published results (Table 2 ). Using the following formula, Sensitivity = m/A, where m is the slope of the calibration plot and A is the geometric surface area of the PIGE, the sensitivity of the N,P-CQD sensor was calculated to be 16.68 μAμM-1cm−2. In addition, the N,P-CQD selectively detect the DA in the analyte mixture made of ascorbic acid, uric acid, and dopamine, thanks to the presence of the various negatively charged functional sites on the CQD surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interference Studies, Reproducibility, and real sample analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Among the three different electrochemical sensing studies, DPV characterization showed a relatively LOD value and thus this characterization tool has been employed for further electrochemical characterization studies. We recorded DPVs for 5 to 60 μM DA in the presence of 1 μM AA and UA as shown in Fig. 10 (i). Next, DPVs were recorded in 0.1 M PBS at pH 7 keeping 1 μM DA concentration constant, and varying AA and UA concentration from 5 to 100 μM as shown in Fig. 10 (ii) and (iii) respectively. N, P-CQD showed an oxidation peak around 0.15 V. However, after the addition of 5 to 100 μM AA and UA into 0.1 M PBS the oxidation peak of DA was not affected, the modified electrode is exclusively selective for DA determination and does not oxidize the acids. The DA oxidation peaks were observed without any change in peak currents but with a very minor shift in oxidation peak up to 0.17 V. We propose that by using N,P-CQD/PIGE modified electrode, it is possible to detect selectively and specifically DA even in the presence of high concentrations of AA and UA. Though earlier reports have been made on the selective detection of DA, those studies involved the use of a glassy carbon electrode coated with Nafion solution [10,44-46]. Contrastingly, the negatively charged functional sites such as acid (–COOH), phosphates (-PO4 3-), and electron-rich nitrogen functionalities (such as pyridinic and pyrrolic groups) selectively attract the cations via., an ion-exchange mechanism leaving behind the anions due to the electrostatic repulsion, thus making it a potential sensing element for selective detection of DA without the aid of Nafion coating.

Repeated analyses of 10 μM DA utilizing three independently modified electrodes were also used to demonstrate the reproducibility of the sensorfor DA detection and the results are consistently similar explained in the ESI. In Table 2, we also juxtaposed the linear range and limit of detection (LOD) of our samples with those of earlier reported results and the LOD values are significantly lower (∼500 pM). Thus, the electrochemical sensor developed using the banana bract-derived N,P-CQD can be a potential candidate for detecting DA neurotransmitters at their lower concentrations.

The prepared N,P-CQD/PIGE electrode was further employed in detecting the real-time samples. Human urine samples spiked with the known concentration of (3 μM) DA was used to assess the practical applicability of our sensor (N,P-CQD). In order to conduct this investigation, CVs were recorded in 0.1 M PBS with the addition of different diluted urine sample quantities. A standard concentration of DA (3 μM) was administered to urine samples of 100, 200, and 300 μL, and CVs were measured. The presence of different quantities of urine samples had no discernible effects on the DA oxidation peak current, which was monitored. This demonstrates that DA detection utilizingelectrodes modified with N,P-CQD is possible in actual samples. The DA concentration that was measured was accurate and within the permitted range. The recovery of the DA was between 101 and 104 %. (Table S2 in the ESI.).

Also, N,P-CQD/PIGE electrode is successfully applied to real-time samples of dopamine hydrochloride injection for DPV and CV analysis, which detects DA with a detection limit as low as ∼ 630 pM with a wider linear range of 2.5 μM to 0.16 mM (R2 value of 0.9972) using DPV method clearly indicating the N,P-CQD modified electrode can be used as a real-time sensor for the analysis of human samples as shown in Fig. S7 in the ESI.

In Summary, novel nitrogen/phosphorus dual-doped carbon quantum dots (N,P-CQD) have been synthesized from banana bract biomass for electrochemical sensing of the dopamine neurotransmitter. Successful formation of ultralow low size (∼3.5 nm) N,P-CQDs with turbostratic nature is corroborated using TEM and XRD. Raman spectroscopic characterization of the prepared CQDs revealed the presence of graphitic carbon with defective sites due to the doping of heteroatoms on the CQD surface. While the doping of the heteroatoms increases the electrical conductivity of the CQD, the presence of the functional sites such as acid (–COOH), (–NH2) and phosphate (-PO4 3-) groups selectively attract the cations via., ion-exchange mechanism leaving behind the anions due to the electrostatic repulsion. The electrochemical sensor fabricated using N,P-CQD coated PIGE electrode was further characterized using various tools such as cyclic voltammetry (CV), chronoamperometry (CA), and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV), which rendered relatively lower detection limits of 613 pM, 3716 pM, and 500 pM, respectively with the wide varying linear ranges depending on the technique employed. Furthermore, the prepared electrode is the first of its kind for the selective detection of dopamine in the mixture of dopamine (DA), uric acid (UA), and ascorbic acid (AA) without the aid of Nafion coating in a PIGE electrode. Moreover, the electrochemical sensor fabricated using N,P-CQD/PIGE electrode detects real-time dopamine hydrochloride with a detection limit as low as ∼ 630 pM with a wider linear range of 2.5 μM to 0.16 mM.
